title: 5.1 Approximating Areas – OpenStax Calculus Volume 1
relevance: Provides clear definitions of Riemann sums, left/right/midpoint approximations, and the link to definite integrals, ideal for Lesson 4.1.

title: 5.2 The Definite Integral – OpenStax Calculus Volume 1
relevance: Explains the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and net area concepts, useful for Lessons 4.2 and 4.4.

heading: Lesson 4.2 – The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
body: The theorem has two parts: (1) If F(x)=∫ₐˣ f(t)dt, then F′(x)=f(x). (2) If F is any antiderivative of f on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b)‑F(a). This bridges differentiation and integration, allowing us to evaluate definite integrals via antiderivatives.

title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1) – Khan Academy
caption: A concise visual proof and intuitive explanation of both parts of the theorem.

questions.q: Using a left‑endpoint Riemann sum with n=4, what is the approximate area under f(x)=x² on [0,2]?
For a linear function f(x)=mx+b, which Riemann sum (left, right, or midpoint) gives the exact area on any interval?
options: All three give the exact area
questions.explanation: Because the area under a straight line is a trapezoid, any uniform rectangle approximation matches the exact integral.

questions.q: As n → ∞, a Riemann sum approaches:
questions.explanation: The limit of Riemann sums is the definition of the definite integral.

title: Explore Riemann Sums
caption: Adjust n, interval, and left/right/midpoint choices to see how the sum approaches the true area.

questions.q: If F(x)=∫₀ˣ sin t dt, what is F′(x)?
questions.explanation: By Part 1 of the FTC, the derivative of the accumulation function equals the integrand: F′(x)=sin x.

questions.q: Given an antiderivative G of f on [a,b], which expression equals ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx?
questions.explanation: Part 2 of the FTC states the definite integral equals the difference of any antiderivative evaluated at the bounds.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the relationship between differentiation and integration?
options: Integration is a special case of differentiation
Differentiation always yields a larger value
questions.explanation: The FTC shows that integrating then differentiating (or vice‑versa) returns the original function, making them inverse operations.

heading: Lesson 4.3 – Basic Antiderivative Rules
body: Key rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1)+C (n≠‑1); constants pull out: ∫k·f(x)dx = k∫f(x)dx; sum rule: ∫[f+g]dx = ∫fdx + ∫gdx; trig: ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C. Worked examples illustrate each rule.

questions.q: Which is an antiderivative of sin x?
questions.explanation: The derivative of -cos x is sin x.
Integrate term‑wise: ∫3x²dx = x³, ∫4dx = 4x, then add constant C.
Derivative of tan x is sec²x, so the antiderivative is tan x + C.

heading: Lesson 4.4 – Applications: Net Change and Simple Volumes
body: The Net‑Change Theorem: ∫ₐᵇ v(t)dt gives total change of a quantity whose rate is v(t). For volumes, the Disk Method uses V = π∫ₐᵇ [R(x)]²dx, where R(x) is the radius of the revolving region.

questions.q: A particle moves with velocity v(t)=3t². What is its displacement from t=0 to t=2?
Find the volume of the solid generated by revolving y=√x about the x‑axis from x=0 to x=4.
questions.explanation: Radius R(x)=√x, so V=π∫₀⁴ (√x)²dx = π∫₀⁴ x dx = π·[x²/2]₀⁴ = π·(16/2)=8π.

questions.q: If the rate of water flow into a tank is r(t)=5 L/min, how much water enters between t=0 and t=10 minutes?

title: Ask the Tutor
persona: You are a tutor for introductory integration. Be concise and Socratic, prompting learners to reason through problems.
greeting: Hi! Ask me anything about integration concepts.
